notes: Arturia CMI V emulates the Fairlight CMI (Computer Musical Instrument), the pioneering Australian digital sampler and synthesizer used by Peter Gabriel, Kate Bush, Trevor Horn, Stevie Wonder, and many others in the early 1980s. The Fairlight combined sample playback with a rudimentary additive synthesis engine (Page R for the sequencer, Page 4 for waveform drawing), producing an immediately recognisable sonic character — bright, slightly harsh, yet musical — that defined the sound of 1980s pop and film scores. Brightness/harmonic content and formant controls are the primary timbral tools, while expression and vibrato shape the performance character of sustained notes.

Expression.description: Expression — scales output level for dynamics; essential for bowed and wind sample patches

Modulation.description: Modulation wheel — typically controls vibrato depth on pitched patches

Brightness.description: Harmonic brightness/filter cutoff — shapes the high-frequency content and air of the sampled/synthesized waveform

Formant.description: Formant control — shifts the spectral centre of the waveform, changing perceived vowel or instrument body character

Attack.description: Sample playback envelope attack — time from note-on to full level

Release.description: Sample playback envelope release — tail length after note off

Vibrato Rate.description: Vibrato rate — speed of pitch modulation applied to sustained notes

Vibrato Depth.description: Vibrato depth — amount of pitch modulation; gives vocal and string patches organic movement

Sample Layer Mix.description: Sample layer mix — balance between layered sample banks or timbral layers within a patch

Reverb.description: Reverb send level — period-correct hall reverb suits the Fairlight's 1980s character

Chorus.description: Chorus depth — adds ensemble width, characteristic of many Fairlight string and pad patches

Sample Tuning.description: Sample tuning/pitch offset — coarse pitch adjustment across the sampled waveform
